content.full_text:
.

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 16, 1993


Honorable Toni Young
Port Hueneme City Council
250 S. Ventura Blvd.
Port Hueneme, CA  93041-2333

Re:  Your Requests for Informal
Assistance; Our Advice File No's.
I-93-316 and I-93-322

Dear Ms. Young:

     This is in response to your requests for assistance under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1/  Since you are seeking general assistance and not
inquiring about a specific decision before you, your letter is
considered a request for informal assistance.2/  In addition, in your
letter of August 17, 1993, you seek advice on the conduct of
another public official.  The Commission can only provide advice
to the public official whose duties are in question under the Act.
(Section 83114.)  We are therefore declining to respond to your
inquiry in that letter.  However, enclosed is a copy of Commission
Regulation 18700.1 which addresses when public officials may
represent their own interests before a governmental agency. This
regulation may provide you with some guidance.

QUESTION

     Will you, as a real estate agent, have a conflict of interest
when voting on property-related issues in the city of Port
Hueneme?


_________________________
1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. I-93-316 and I-93-322
Page Two


CONCLUSION

Since you are a real estate agent, the broker or brokerage
business with whom you work, the person you represent and any
person who receives a finder's fee in a real estate transaction,
will be sources of income to you. You may not participate in
decisions which will have a material financial effect on any
source of income of $250 or more received, or promised, during the
12 months preceding a decision by the city council.

FACTS

You have not provided any specific facts relative to any
decision currently before the city council. You are a Port
Hueneme City Councilmember and a real estate agent and want to
know when you will have a conflict of interest.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family, or on:

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(c) and (d)
Emphasis added

Advice File No. I-93-312 and I-93-322
Page Three


     "Income" includes a payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, or gift. (Section 8203O.)

     In real estate transactions, the sources of commission income to a real estate agent are:

          (A)  The broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the agent works;

          (B)  The person the agent represents in the transaction; and

          (C)  Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for referring a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral pursuant to a contract with the broker.

                              (Regulation 18704.3(c)(3).)

     In real estate transactions, income from the sources listed above is attributable to you once it is "promised." Income is promised once a sale is pending on the property, i.e., the property is in escrow.3/  (Felts Advice Letter, No. A-85-130.)

     Once a sale on the property is pending, all of the parties to the transaction identified in Regulation 18704.3(c)(3) above, will be considered a source of income to you for the full value of the commission. (Regulation 18704.2(d).) You may not make, participate in making, or attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable4/ material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on your source of income.


_______________________

3/  We have previously held that "multiple listings" also constitute "promised income" regardless of whether a sale on any one property was pending. However, upon reconsideration, it appears that holding a multiple listing does not provide any greater assurance of receiving income. This conclusion supersedes advice letters that hold otherwise, including the Felts Advice Letter, No. A-85-130 and Remelmeyer Advice Letter, No. A-81-510.

4/  Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Advice File No. I-93-312 and I-93-322
Page Four


Material Financial Effect

Business Entities or Individuals "Directly" Affected by the
Decision

    To determine when a decision will have a material financial
effect on an economic interest, you refer to Commission
regulations for guidance. Regulation 18702.1 provides that the
effect of a decision will be material on any source of income or
business entity, if the entity or source is directly involved in
the decision.

    A person or entity is directly involved in a decision before
your agency when that person or entity, either personally or by an
agent:

          (1)  Initiates the proceeding in which the decision
will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or
similar request or;

          (2)  Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official
or the official's agency.

          (3)  A person or business entity is the subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person or business entity.

               (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

    If, for example, the person or entity you represented in a
transaction files an application before the city to rezone
property, or requests a special use permit, that person or entity
is "directly" before the city council. The decision concerning
that property or permit is deemed to have a material financial
effect on your source of income and you could not participate in
that decision.

Business Entities "Indirectly" Affected by the Decision

    You may also have a conflict of interest if your source of
income is indirectly material affect. For example, if the source
of income is a business entity indirectly involved in the
decision, Regulation 18702.2, (Copy enclosed) establishes monetary
thresholds to determine when the decision will have a material
financial effect on the business entity. For small business
entities, Regulation 18702.2 provides that a decision will have a
material financial effect on the entity if:

Advice File No. I-93-312 and I-93-322
Page Five


          (1)  The decision will result in an increase or
     decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
     $10,000 or more; or

          (2)  The decision will result in the business
     entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
     reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
     year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

          (3)  The decision will result in an increase or
     decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
     $10,000 or more.

                         (Regulation 18702.2(g).)

     For example, this regulation would apply to the brokerage
firm where you work or any client who is a business entity.

     After you have determined the fiscal size of a business
entity, you apply the appropriate standard from the regulation to
determine if a decision will have a material financial effect on
the entity.  For example, if the standard for small business
entities was applicable, you would be prohibited from
participating in decisions of the city council regarding
improvements to water or sewer lines on a street where your
business entity client owned property, if the decision affected
the assets of the business entity, (including their property,) by
$10,000 or more.

Individuals "Indirectly" Affected by the Decision

     If the source of income is an individual, Regulation 18702.6
provides that the decision will be material if:

          (a)  The decision will affect the individual's
     income, investments or other intangible or intangible
     assets or liabilities (other than real property) by
     $1,000 or more; or

          (b)  The decision will affect the individual's real
     property interest in a manner that is considered
     material under Section 18702.3 or Section 18702.4.4/


_______________________
4/  Sections 18702.3 and 18702.4 are Commission regulations which
establish the material financial effect on real property based on
the distance the property is from the property which is the
subject of the decision.  Regulations 18702.3 and 18702.4 are
enclosed.

Advice File No. I-93-312 and I-93-322
Page Six


     For this example, if the city council were to consider street
improvements for property located within 300 feet of the property
owned by an individual who has been a source of income to you,
that decision would have a material financial effect on your,
source of income and you could not participate in that decision,
unless the decision will have no financial effect on the
individual's real property interest.  (Regulation 18702.3(a).)

     I hope this has provided you with some guidance on potential
conflicts of interest.  When there is a specific decision before
the city council, we suggest you contact the Commission for
advice.  All requests for formal advice will be responded to in 21
working days after all of the facts pertinent to the question have
been provided.  (Regulation 18329.)  If you have any questions,
please feel free to contact the Commission's Legal Division at
(916) 322-5901.

                              Sincerely,

                              [illegible]
                              Jeanette E. Turvill
                              Political Reform Consultant
                              Legal Division

JET/jt
Enclosures
